_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Maury County, TN. Formed 1807. No major courthouse fire; records survive from formation. Excellent early records reflecting the county's antebellum wealth.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame and then brick courthouse. Replaced as the county's prosperity demanded a more substantial building.
Substantial brick courthouse reflecting the county's antebellum wealth. Survived the Civil War. Replaced by the current 1904 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or destruction
record_losses.description: Maury County's courthouse has not suffered a major fire, and the Civil War — though devastating to the county's economy and population — did not result in record destruction. The county's antebellum wealth ensured meticulous record-keeping, particularly in estate records where enslaved people were documented as property. The Spring Hill-Franklin-Nashville corridor saw heavy military activity in late 1864, but Columbia was not the site of a major engagement. Records run continuously from the 1807 formation.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Maury County's records survive intact from 1807 — if {ancestor_name} lived here, the documentary trail is as complete as Tennessee offers. The county's wealth meant that property transfers, estate settlements, and court proceedings were carefully recorded.
record_losses.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records.

deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Maury County's deed records are particularly valuable for documenting the plantation economy.
deeds.repository: Maury County Register of Deeds; Tennessee State Library and Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Tennessee land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — estate records reflect the county's plantation wealth in detail
wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest loose papers. Bound volumes complete. Estate inventories frequently list enslaved people by name, age, and sometimes family relationship.
wills_estates.repository: Maury County Probate Court; Tennessee State Library and Archives

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds from formation; licenses from 1838
marriages.gaps: Some gaps in the earliest bond records.
marriages.repository: Maury County Clerk; Tennessee State Library and Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous series with minor wartime disruption
court_minutes.gaps: Some disruption during Civil War years reflecting government interruption.

substitute_sources.description: Phosphate mining companies operating in Maury County (1890s-1940s) maintained employment records, payroll ledgers, and accident reports. Where these records survive, they document workers — predominantly Black men — who might appear nowhere else in the county records.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee State Library and Archives; limited company archives; some at University of Tennessee Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Potentially valuable for identifying Black workers in the phosphate industry. Mining company payrolls may be the only record of some workers' presence in the county between census years.
substitute_sources.source: Hayes, C. Willard. 'The Tennessee Phosphates.' USGS Twenty-Second Annual Report, 1902.

substitute_sources.description: The investigation and trial records from the 1946 Columbia Race Riot document names, addresses, and relationships within the Black community. The NAACP defense records (Thurgood Marshall's files) are also relevant.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee State Library and Archives; NAACP Papers at Library of Congress; National Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The arrest records, trial transcripts, and newspaper coverage document the Black community in Columbia in extraordinary detail — names, occupations, addresses, and family relationships of over 100 individuals.
substitute_sources.source: Egerton, John. Speak Now Against the Day. Knopf, 1994.

substitute_sources.description: Maury County's churches (Presbyterian, Methodist, Baptist, and after 1865, Black Baptist and AME) maintained records. Cemetery transcriptions by the Maury County Historical Society provide death dates and family relationships.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Maury County Historical Society; Tennessee State Library and Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Supplement civil records for vital events before 1908.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to Church Records.

narrative_hooks: Maury County's records survive intact from 1807 — {ancestor_name}'s property transactions, estate settlements, and court appearances are almost certainly documented in unbroken series.

If {ancestor_name} was enslaved in Maury County, the estate inventories may list {pronoun_object} by name — Maury County's wealthy planters died with detailed inventories of everything they owned, including the people they held in bondage.

The Columbia Race Riot of 1946 generated arrest records that document Black community members by name, address, and occupation — if {ancestor_name}'s family was in Columbia in 1946, the riot records may be the most detailed snapshot of the Black community that exists.
If {ancestor_name} worked the phosphate mines, the company payroll may be the only record of {pronoun_possessive} existence between census years — the mines kept books, even when the county did not.
source_references: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records in the Tennessee State Library and Archives. Nashville: TSLA.
Hayes, C. Willard. 'The Tennessee Phosphates.' Twenty-Second Annual Report USGS, 1902.
Egerton, John. Speak Now Against the Day. New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 1994.
